title: Die Gänsemagd
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 2, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 89. Text from Wikisource („Die Gänsemagd (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The eighty-ninth tale (KHM 89). A princess sent to be married is robbed of her place by a treacherous waiting-maid; set to herd geese, she is betrayed even by the speaking head of her horse Falada, until the old king uncovers the truth and the false bride pronounces her own grisly sentence. Verse refrains recur; 1857 spelling is kept (Thor, Räthsel, Urtheil, werth).

sentences.t: Es lebte einmal eine alte Königin, der war ihr Gemahl schon lange Jahre gestorben, und sie hatte eine schöne Tochter.
sentences.l: There lived once an old queen, whose consort had already died many long years ago, and she had a beautiful daughter.
sentences.i: There once lived an old queen whose husband had died many long years before, and she had a beautiful daughter.
sentences.n: “der war ihr Gemahl … gestorben” — a dative-of-possession relative: literally “to whom her consort had died,” i.e. whose husband had died. German often marks possession with the dative rather than a genitive.

sentences.t: Wie die erwuchs, wurde sie weit über Feld an einen Königssohn versprochen.
sentences.l: As she grew up, she was promised far across country to a king’s son.
sentences.i: As she grew up, she was betrothed to a king’s son far away across the land.
sentences.n: “wie” here means “as / when” (temporal), a very common Grimm usage. “versprechen” in this marriage sense = to promise in betrothal, to betroth.

sentences.t: Als nun die Zeit kam, wo sie vermählt werden sollten und das Kind in das fremde Reich abreisen mußte, packte ihr die Alte gar viel köstliches Geräth und Geschmeide ein, Gold und Silber, Becher und Kleinode, kurz alles, was nur zu einem königlichen Brautschatz gehörte, denn sie hatte ihr Kind von Herzen lieb.
sentences.l: When now the time came, when they were to be wedded and the child had to depart into the foreign realm, the old woman packed for her very much costly ware and jewellery, gold and silver, cups and trinkets, in short everything that only belonged to a royal bride’s treasure, for she had her child dear from the heart.
sentences.i: When the time came for them to be married and the child had to set off for the foreign kingdom, the old queen packed up for her all manner of costly furnishings and jewellery — gold and silver, goblets and trinkets, in short everything that belonged to a royal dowry, for she loved her child dearly.
sentences.n: 1857 spelling: Geräth (modern Gerät). Kleinode = precious trinkets, jewels (plural of Kleinod). “einpacken” is a separable verb — the prefix ein- lands at the end (“packte … ein”); you’ll see this pattern constantly. “von Herzen lieb haben” = to love dearly, from the heart. Brautschatz = the bride’s treasure, her dowry.

sentences.t: Auch gab sie ihr eine Kammerjungfer bei, welche mitreiten und die Braut in die Hände des Bräutigams überliefern sollte, und jede bekam ein Pferd zur Reise, aber das Pferd der Königstochter hieß Falada und konnte sprechen.
sentences.l: Also she gave her a chambermaid along, who was to ride with her and deliver the bride into the hands of the bridegroom, and each received a horse for the journey, but the horse of the king’s daughter was called Falada and could speak.
sentences.i: She also sent along a waiting-maid, who was to ride with her and deliver the bride into the hands of the bridegroom; and each was given a horse for the journey, but the king’s daughter’s horse was named Falada and could speak.
sentences.n: “beigeben” (separable, prefix bei-) = to assign, send along with someone. Falada is a proper name — left untranslated throughout; the talking horse is a folk-tale motif.

sentences.t: Wie nun die Abschiedsstunde da war, begab sich die alte Mutter in ihre Schlafkammer, nahm ein Messerlein und schnitt damit in ihre Finger, daß sie bluteten: darauf hielt sie ein weißes Läppchen unter und ließ drei Tropfen Blut hineinfallen, gab sie der Tochter und sprach „liebes Kind, verwahre sie wohl, sie werden dir unterweges Noth thun.“
sentences.l: When now the hour of parting was there, the old mother betook herself into her sleeping-chamber, took a little knife and cut with it into her fingers, so that they bled: thereupon she held a little white cloth underneath and let three drops of blood fall into it, gave them to the daughter and spoke “dear child, keep them well, they will be needful to you on the way.”
sentences.i: When the hour of parting came, the old mother went into her bedchamber, took a little knife and cut her fingers with it until they bled; then she held a little white cloth beneath and let three drops of blood fall onto it, gave them to her daughter and said: “Dear child, guard them well — they will stand you in good stead on the way.”
sentences.n: Diminutives -lein: Messerlein (little knife), Läppchen (little cloth). “sich begeben” = to betake oneself, go. Archaic: thun (modern tun), Noth (Not), unterweges (unterwegs). “Noth thun” = to be needful, to be of use — “dir Noth thun” = to serve you in your need. The three drops of blood are the tale’s central talisman.

sentences.t: Also nahmen beide von einander betrübten Abschied: das Läppchen steckte die Königstochter in ihren Busen vor sich, setzte sich aufs Pferd und zog nun fort zu ihrem Bräutigam.
sentences.l: So both took a grieved leave of one another: the little cloth the king’s daughter tucked into her bosom before her, sat herself upon the horse and now went off to her bridegroom.
sentences.i: So the two took a sorrowful leave of one another; the king’s daughter tucked the little cloth into her bosom, mounted her horse, and set off for her bridegroom.
sentences.n: “Abschied nehmen” = to take one’s leave. Note the fronted object “das Läppchen … in ihren Busen”: German lets you put the object first for emphasis, then the verb and subject follow (“steckte die Königstochter”).

sentences.t: Da sie eine Stunde geritten waren, empfand sie heißen Durst und sprach zu ihrer Kammerjungfer „steig ab, und schöpfe mir mit meinem Becher, den du für mich mitgenommen hast, Wasser aus dem Bache, ich möchte gern einmal trinken.“
sentences.l: When they had ridden an hour, she felt hot thirst and spoke to her chambermaid “get down, and scoop for me with my cup, which you have taken along for me, water out of the brook, I should like to drink once.”
sentences.i: When they had ridden an hour, she grew very thirsty and said to her waiting-maid: “Get down and scoop me some water from the brook with my cup that you carried for me — I should so like a drink.”
sentences.n: “absteigen” (separable) = to dismount; imperative “steig ab.” “schöpfen” = to scoop, draw (water). Bache is the dative of Bach (brook). “möchte gern” = would like to.

sentences.t: „Wenn ihr Durst habt,“ sprach die Kammerjungfer, „so steigt selber ab, legt euch ans Wasser und trinkt, ich mag eure Magd nicht sein.“
sentences.l: “If you have thirst,” spoke the chambermaid, “then get down yourself, lay yourself by the water and drink, I do not want to be your maid.”
sentences.i: “If you’re thirsty,” said the waiting-maid, “then get down yourself, lie by the water and drink — I have no mind to be your servant.”
sentences.n: The maid addresses the princess with the plural ihr / euch (an older polite/formal “you”), yet her words are insolent — the tension between form and tone marks her rebellion. “mögen” here = to want, care to.

sentences.t: Da stieg die Königstochter vor großem Durst herunter, neigte sich über das Wasser im Bach und trank, und durfte nicht aus dem goldnen Becher trinken.
sentences.l: Then the king’s daughter climbed down for great thirst, bent herself over the water in the brook and drank, and was not allowed to drink out of the golden cup.
sentences.i: Then, for her great thirst, the king’s daughter climbed down, leaned over the water in the brook and drank, and was not permitted to drink from the golden cup.
sentences.n: “vor großem Durst” — vor + dative here gives the cause: “out of / because of great thirst.” goldnen is a contracted form of goldenen (golden).

sentences.t: Da sprach sie „ach Gott!“ da antworteten die drei Blutstropfen „wenn das deine Mutter wüßte, das Herz im Leibe thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.l: Then she spoke “ah God!” then answered the three blood-drops “if your mother knew that, the heart in her body would burst.”
sentences.i: Then she said, “Ah, God!” and the three drops of blood answered, “If your mother knew this, the heart in her body would break.”
sentences.n: The refrain of the blood-drops. “wüßte” and “thät … zerspringen” are subjunctives of a contrary-to-fact wish: “if she knew … it would burst.” thät (modern täte) forms an archaic würde-like conditional. im Leibe = in her body (Leib, old dative -e).

sentences.t: Aber die Königsbraut war demüthig, sagte nichts und stieg wieder zu Pferd.
sentences.l: But the king’s bride was humble, said nothing and got again to horse.
sentences.i: But the king’s bride was meek; she said nothing and mounted her horse again.
sentences.n: 1857 spelling demüthig (modern demütig). “zu Pferd steigen” = to mount, get on horseback.

sentences.t: So ritten sie etliche Meilen weiter fort, aber der Tag war warm, die Sonne stach, und sie durstete bald von neuem.
sentences.l: So they rode several miles further on, but the day was warm, the sun stung, and she soon thirsted anew.
sentences.i: So they rode on several miles further, but the day was warm, the sun beat down, and she soon grew thirsty again.
sentences.n: “etliche” = several, a good number of. “dursten” (impersonal-ish here, sie durstete) = to be thirsty. “von neuem” = anew, afresh.

sentences.t: Da sie nun an einen Wasserfluß kamen, rief sie noch einmal ihrer Kammerjungfer „steig ab und gib mir aus meinem Goldbecher zu trinken,“ denn sie hatte aller bösen Worte längst vergessen.
sentences.l: When they now came to a watercourse, she called once more to her chambermaid “get down and give me out of my gold-cup to drink,” for she had long since forgotten all the wicked words.
sentences.i: When they came to a stream, she called once more to her waiting-maid, “Get down and give me a drink from my gold cup,” for she had long since forgotten all those spiteful words.
sentences.n: “rufen” with the dative (ihrer Kammerjungfer) = to call to someone. “aller bösen Worte vergessen” — vergessen with a genitive object (aller Worte) is an older, elevated construction; modern German would use the accusative.

sentences.t: Die Kammerjungfer sprach aber noch hochmüthiger, „wollt ihr trinken, so trinkt allein, ich mag nicht eure Magd sein.“
sentences.l: But the chambermaid spoke still more haughtily, “if you want to drink, then drink alone, I do not want to be your maid.”
sentences.i: But the waiting-maid spoke even more haughtily: “If you want to drink, drink by yourself — I have no mind to be your servant.”
sentences.n: hochmüthiger (1857; modern hochmütiger) = more haughty. “wollt ihr trinken, so trinkt” — a conditional without wenn: fronting the verb (“want you to drink”) is itself the “if.”

sentences.t: Da stieg die Königstochter hernieder vor großem Durst, legte sich über das fließende Wasser, weinte und sprach „ach Gott!“ und die Blutstropfen antworteten wiederum „wenn das deine Mutter wüßte, das Herz im Leibe thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.l: Then the king’s daughter climbed down for great thirst, laid herself over the flowing water, wept and spoke “ah God!” and the blood-drops answered again “if your mother knew that, the heart in her body would burst.”
sentences.i: Then, for her great thirst, the king’s daughter climbed down, leaned over the running water, wept and said, “Ah, God!” — and the drops of blood answered again, “If your mother knew this, the heart in her body would break.”
sentences.n: hernieder = down, downward (archaic/elevated). wiederum = again, in turn. The refrain returns unchanged — its repetition is the tale’s slow warning.

sentences.t: Und wie sie so trank und sich recht überlehnte, fiel ihr das Läppchen, worin die drei Tropfen waren, aus dem Busen und floß mit dem Wasser fort ohne daß sie es in ihrer großen Angst merkte.
sentences.l: And as she so drank and leaned right over, the little cloth, wherein the three drops were, fell out of her bosom and floated away with the water without her noticing it in her great fear.
sentences.i: And as she drank, leaning far out, the little cloth with the three drops in it fell from her bosom and floated off with the water, without her noticing it in her great distress.
sentences.n: “sich überlehnen” = to lean out over (an unusual verb; überlehnte, separable-ish here). “worin” = wherein, in which. “fiel ihr das Läppchen” — dative ihr of disadvantage: the cloth fell from her, on her. Losing the drops strips her of protection — the pivot of the tale.

sentences.t: Die Kammerjungfer hatte aber zugesehen und freute sich daß sie Gewalt über die Braut bekäme: denn damit, daß diese die Blutstropfen verloren hatte, war sie schwach und machtlos geworden.
sentences.l: But the chambermaid had watched and rejoiced that she would get power over the bride: for by this, that the latter had lost the blood-drops, she had become weak and powerless.
sentences.i: But the waiting-maid had been watching, and rejoiced that she would gain power over the bride: for now that the girl had lost the drops of blood, she had become weak and powerless.
sentences.n: “zusehen” (separable) = to look on, watch. “Gewalt über … bekommen” = to gain power over. diese (“this one, the latter”) refers to the princess; German uses the demonstrative to track who is meant.

sentences.t: Als sie nun wieder auf ihr Pferd steigen wollte, das da hieß Falada, sagte die Kammerfrau „auf Falada gehör ich, und auf meinen Gaul gehörst du;“ und das mußte sie sich gefallen lassen.
sentences.l: When she now wanted to climb again onto her horse, which was called Falada, the chamber-woman said “onto Falada I belong, and onto my nag you belong;” and that she had to let herself be content with.
sentences.i: When she was about to mount her horse again — the one called Falada — the waiting-woman said, “On Falada is where I belong, and on my nag is where you belong”; and she had to put up with it.
sentences.n: Gaul = nag, workhorse (a plain or poor horse, opposed to the noble Falada). “sich (etwas) gefallen lassen” = to put up with, submit to something. Kammerfrau / Kammerjungfer / Kammermädchen are all used for the false maid.

sentences.t: Dann befahl ihr die Kammerfrau mit harten Worten die königlichen Kleider auszuziehen und ihre schlechten anzulegen, und endlich mußte sie sich unter freiem Himmel verschwören daß sie am königlichen Hof keinem Menschen etwas davon sprechen wollte; und wenn sie diesen Eid nicht abgelegt hätte, wäre sie auf der Stelle umgebracht worden.
sentences.l: Then the chamber-woman commanded her with hard words to take off the royal clothes and to put on her poor ones, and finally she had to swear herself under the open sky that she would speak to no person at the royal court anything of it; and if she had not sworn this oath, she would have been killed on the spot.
sentences.i: Then the waiting-woman harshly ordered her to take off the royal garments and put on the maid’s poor ones, and at last she had to swear under the open sky that she would say nothing of this to a soul at the royal court; and had she not sworn this oath, she would have been killed on the spot.
sentences.n: Two separable infinitives: “ausziehen” (take off) and “anlegen” (put on), each split with zu- (auszuziehen, anzulegen). “sich verschwören” = to swear a solemn oath. “einen Eid ablegen” = to take an oath. “auf der Stelle” = on the spot, at once. schlechten here = poor, mean (of clothes), not “bad” in the modern sense.

sentences.t: Aber Falada sah das alles an und nahms wohl in Acht.
sentences.l: But Falada looked at all that and took it well into heed.
sentences.i: But Falada saw all this and marked it well.
sentences.n: “ansehen” (separable) = to look at, observe. nahms = nahm es (took it), a colloquial contraction. “in Acht nehmen” = to take note of, mark, heed.

sentences.t: Die Kammerfrau stieg nun auf Falada und die wahre Braut auf das schlechte Roß, und so zogen sie weiter, bis sie endlich in dem königlichen Schloß eintrafen.
sentences.l: The chamber-woman now climbed onto Falada and the true bride onto the poor steed, and so they went further, until at last they arrived at the royal castle.
sentences.i: The waiting-woman now mounted Falada and the true bride the sorry nag, and so they went on until at last they reached the royal castle.
sentences.n: Roß = steed (here ironically the poor horse). “eintreffen” (separable) = to arrive.

sentences.t: Da war große Freude über ihre Ankunft, und der Königssohn sprang ihnen entgegen, hob die Kammerfrau vom Pferde und meinte sie wäre seine Gemahlin: sie ward die Treppe hinaufgeführt, die wahre Königstochter aber mußte unten stehen bleiben.
sentences.l: There was great joy over their arrival, and the king’s son sprang toward them, lifted the chamber-woman from the horse and thought she was his consort: she was led up the stairs, but the true king’s daughter had to remain standing below.
sentences.i: There was great joy at their arrival, and the king’s son ran to meet them, lifted the waiting-woman from her horse thinking she was his bride; she was led up the staircase, but the true king’s daughter had to stay standing below.
sentences.n: “entgegenspringen” (separable, with dative ihnen) = to leap toward, run to meet. ward = archaic preterite of werden (became / was), here forming a passive: “ward … hinaufgeführt” = was led up. “meinen” = to think, suppose.

sentences.t: Da schaute der alte König am Fenster, und sah sie im Hof halten und sah wie sie fein war, zart und gar schön: gieng alsbald hin ins königliche Gemach und fragte die Braut nach der, die sie bei sich hätte und da unten im Hofe stände, und wer sie wäre?
sentences.l: Then the old king looked at the window, and saw her holding in the courtyard and saw how fine she was, delicate and right beautiful: went at once thither into the royal chamber and asked the bride about the one whom she had with her and who stood down there in the courtyard, and who she was?
sentences.i: Then the old king looked out of the window and saw her waiting in the courtyard, and saw how fine she was, delicate and truly lovely; he went at once into the royal chamber and asked the bride about the girl she had with her, who was standing down in the courtyard, and who she might be.
sentences.n: Archaic gieng (modern ging). Gemach = chamber, room (elevated). gar = quite, truly (intensifier). “nach der … die” = about the one who; hätte, stände, wäre are subjunctives of reported/indirect question — “whom she said she had,” “who she might be.”

sentences.t: „Die hab ich mir unterwegs mitgenommen zur Gesellschaft; gebt der Magd was zu arbeiten, daß sie nicht müßig steht.“
sentences.l: “That one I took along with me on the way for company; give the maid something to work, that she does not stand idle.”
sentences.i: “That one I picked up along the way for company; give the maid some work to do, so she doesn’t stand about idle.”
sentences.n: “mitnehmen” (separable) = to take along. was = colloquial for etwas (something). müßig = idle. gebt is the plural imperative (formal ihr).

sentences.t: Aber der alte König hatte keine Arbeit für sie und wußte nichts, als daß er sagte „da hab ich so einen kleinen Jungen, der hütet die Gänse, dem mag sie helfen.“
sentences.l: But the old king had no work for her and knew nothing except that he said “there I have such a little boy, who tends the geese, him she may help.”
sentences.i: But the old king had no work for her and could think of nothing except to say, “I have a little boy here who tends the geese — she may help him.”
sentences.n: “nichts, als daß” = nothing except that. “Gänse hüten” = to tend/herd geese (Gans, plural Gänse). “dem mag sie helfen” — helfen takes the dative (dem = him).

sentences.t: Der Junge hieß Kürdchen (Conrädchen), dem mußte die wahre Braut helfen Gänse hüten.
sentences.l: The boy was called Kürdchen (little-Conrad), him the true bride had to help tend geese.
sentences.i: The boy was called Kürdchen (little Conrad), and the true bride had to help him tend the geese.
sentences.n: Kürdchen is a dialect diminutive of Conrad; Grimm glosses it with the standard form Conrädchen. Both are proper names (left untranslated). This is where “die Gänsemagd,” the goose-girl of the title, gets her name.

sentences.t: Bald aber sprach die falsche Braut zu dem jungen König „liebster Gemahl, ich bitte euch thut mir einen Gefallen.“
sentences.l: But soon the false bride spoke to the young king “dearest consort, I beg you do me a favour.”
sentences.i: But soon the false bride said to the young king, “Dearest husband, I beg you, do me a favour.”
sentences.n: liebster = dearest (superlative of lieb). “thut” (modern tut) is the plural imperative of thun/tun; she addresses him with the polite euch. “einen Gefallen thun” = to do a favour.

sentences.t: Er antwortete „das will ich gerne thun.“
sentences.l: He answered “that I will gladly do.”
sentences.i: He answered, “That I will gladly do.”

sentences.t: „Nun so laßt den Schinder rufen und da dem Pferde, worauf ich hergeritten bin, den Hals abhauen, weil es mich unterweges geärgert hat.“
sentences.l: “Now then let the knacker be called and there the horse, on which I have ridden here, its neck hewn off, because it has vexed me on the way.”
sentences.i: “Then have the knacker sent for, and let the horse I rode here have its neck cut through, because it vexed me on the way.”
sentences.n: Schinder = knacker, the man who slaughters and flays worn-out horses (a despised trade). “den Hals abhauen” = to cut off / hew through the neck (abhauen, separable). “laßt … rufen … abhauen” = have (someone) called, have (the neck) cut — causative lassen. hergeritten = ridden here (her- = hither).

sentences.t: Eigentlich aber fürchtete sie daß das Pferd sprechen möchte wie sie mit der Königstochter umgegangen war.
sentences.l: But really she feared that the horse might speak how she had dealt with the king’s daughter.
sentences.i: But in truth she feared the horse might tell how she had treated the king’s daughter.
sentences.n: “umgehen mit” (separable; here past participle umgegangen) = to deal with, treat (someone). möchte = might (subjunctive of mögen).

sentences.t: Nun war das so weit gerathen, daß es geschehen und der treue Falada sterben sollte, da kam es auch der rechten Königstochter zu Ohr, und sie versprach dem Schinder heimlich ein Stück Geld, das sie ihm bezahlen wollte, wenn er ihr einen kleinen Dienst erwiese.
sentences.l: Now it had come so far that it should happen and the faithful Falada should die, then it also came to the true king’s daughter’s ear, and she secretly promised the knacker a piece of money, which she would pay him, if he would render her a small service.
sentences.i: Now things had come so far that it was to be done and faithful Falada was to die; word of it reached the true king’s daughter too, and she secretly promised the knacker a bit of money, to be paid him if he would do her a small service.
sentences.n: gerathen (1857; modern geraten) = to turn out, come to a pass. “zu Ohr kommen” = to reach someone’s ears. erwiese = subjunctive of erweisen (to render, do a service) — the conditional “if he would.” “ein Stück Geld” = a piece / bit of money.

sentences.t: In der Stadt war ein großes finsteres Thor, wo sie Abends und Morgens mit den Gänsen durch mußte, „unter das finstere Thor möchte er dem Falada seinen Kopf hinnageln, daß sie ihn doch noch mehr als einmal sehen könnte.“
sentences.l: In the town was a great dark gate, where she had to go through evenings and mornings with the geese, “under the dark gate might he nail Falada’s head for her, so that she could yet see him more than once.”
sentences.i: In the town there was a great dark gateway, where she had to pass through mornings and evenings with the geese; would he nail Falada’s head up under the dark gate, she asked, so that she might still see him more than once.
sentences.n: 1857 Thor (modern Tor, gate). “durch mußte” = had to go through (with the motion verb “gehen” understood after müssen). “hinnageln” (separable) = to nail up. möchte here is the polite request-subjunctive: “would he please nail…”. dem Falada seinen Kopf = Falada’s head (dative-possessive: “to Falada his head”).

sentences.t: Also versprach das der Schindersknecht zu thun, hieb den Kopf ab und nagelte ihn unter das finstere Thor fest.
sentences.l: So the knacker’s lad promised to do that, hewed the head off and nailed it fast under the dark gate.
sentences.i: So the knacker’s man promised to do it, struck off the head, and nailed it fast beneath the dark gateway.
sentences.n: Schindersknecht = the knacker’s servant/lad (Knecht = farmhand, man). “abhauen” (here preterite hieb … ab) = to hew off; “festnageln” (nagelte … fest) = to nail fast — both separable.

sentences.t: Des Morgens früh, da sie und Kürdchen unterm Thor hinaus trieben, sprach sie im Vorbeigehen
sentences.l: Early in the morning, when she and Kürdchen drove out under the gate, she spoke in passing
sentences.i: Early in the morning, as she and Kürdchen drove the geese out under the gate, she said in passing:
sentences.n: “des Morgens” = in the morning (genitive of time). unterm = unter dem. “hinaustreiben” (separable) = to drive out. “im Vorbeigehen” = in passing, as she went by.

sentences.t: „o du Falada, da du hangest,“
sentences.l: “O you Falada, where you hang,”
sentences.n: The goose-girl’s greeting to the severed head. “da” here is the old relative “where / as”; hangest is the archaic 2nd-person of hängen (you hang). The rhyme hangest / gangest pairs across her line and the head’s reply.

sentences.t: da antwortete der Kopf
sentences.l: then the head answered

sentences.t:
„o du Jungfer Königin, da du gangest,
wenn das deine Mutter wüßte,
ihr Herz thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.n: The dead horse’s answering rhyme. Jungfer = maiden, young unmarried woman. gangest = archaic 2nd-person of gehen (you go/walk), rhyming with hangest. thät … zerspringen = would burst — the same contrary-to-fact subjunctive as the blood-drops’ refrain, now spoken by Falada. Note this first version has ihr Herz (“her heart”); later repetitions vary it to das Herz.

sentences.t: Da zog sie still weiter zur Stadt hinaus, und sie trieben die Gänse aufs Feld.
sentences.l: Then she went on quietly out of the town, and they drove the geese onto the field.
sentences.i: Then she went quietly on out of the town, and they drove the geese out to the meadow.
sentences.n: “hinausziehen” (separable) = to go/move out. aufs = auf das.

sentences.t: Und wenn sie auf der Wiese angekommen war, saß sie nieder und machte ihre Haare auf, die waren eitel Gold, und Kürdchen sah sie und freute sich wie sie glänzten, und wollte ihr ein paar ausraufen.
sentences.l: And when she had arrived on the meadow, she sat down and undid her hair, which was pure gold, and Kürdchen saw it and rejoiced how it shone, and wanted to pluck out a few for himself.
sentences.i: And when she had reached the meadow, she sat down and let her hair down — it was pure gold — and Kürdchen saw it and delighted in how it shone, and wanted to pluck a few out.
sentences.n: “niedersitzen” (nieder = down) and “aufmachen” (to undo, open) are separable. eitel here is the old sense “pure, sheer, nothing but,” not the modern “vain.” “ausraufen” = to pluck/tear out.

sentences.t: Da sprach sie
sentences.l: Then she spoke

sentences.t:
„weh, weh, Windchen,
nimm Kürdchen sein Hütchen,
und laß’n sich mit jagen,
bis ich mich geflochten und geschnatzt,
und wieder aufgesatzt.“
sentences.l:
“blow, blow, little-wind,
take Kürdchen his little-hat,
and make him chase after,
till I have braided and plaited my hair,
and set it up again.”
sentences.i:
“Blow, blow, little wind,
catch hold of Kürdchen’s hat,
and set him chasing after it,
till I have braided and bound my hair,
and put it up again.”
sentences.n: The wind-charm. weh is the imperative of wehen (to blow) — a pun on weh = “woe.” Windchen, Hütchen are diminutives. “Kürdchen sein Hütchen” = Kürdchen’s little hat (dative-possessive, colloquial). laß’n = laß ihn (let/make him); “sich mit jagen” = go chasing along after it. geschnatzt (dialect) and aufgesatzt (dialect for aufgesetzt) = braided/plaited and put up (of hair) — she keeps her golden hair from Kürdchen’s grasp.

sentences.t: Und da kam ein so starker Wind, daß er dem Kürdchen sein Hütchen weg wehte über alle Land, und es mußte ihm nachlaufen.
sentences.l: And then came such a strong wind that it blew Kürdchen’s little hat away over all the country, and he had to run after it.
sentences.i: And then came so strong a wind that it blew Kürdchen’s hat away over all the fields, and he had to run after it.
sentences.n: “wegwehen” (separable) = to blow away. “dem Kürdchen sein Hütchen” = Kürdchen’s hat (dative-possessive again). “nachlaufen” (separable, + dative ihm) = to run after. Note es for Kürdchen — a diminutive is grammatically neuter, so the boy is “it.”

sentences.t: Bis es wieder kam war sie mit dem Kämmen und Aufsetzen fertig, und er konnte keine Haare kriegen.
sentences.l: By the time he came back she was finished with the combing and putting-up, and he could get no hairs.
sentences.i: By the time he came back, she had finished combing and putting up her hair, and he could get none of it.
sentences.n: Verbal nouns (capitalized infinitives): das Kämmen (combing), das Aufsetzen (putting up). “fertig mit” = finished with. kriegen = to get (colloquial).

sentences.t: Da war Kürdchen bös und sprach nicht mit ihr; und so hüteten sie die Gänse bis daß es Abend ward, dann giengen sie nach Haus.
sentences.l: Then Kürdchen was angry and did not speak with her; and so they tended the geese until it became evening, then they went home.
sentences.i: Then Kürdchen was cross and would not speak to her; and so they tended the geese until evening came, and then went home.
sentences.n: bös = angry, cross. “bis daß” = until (the daß is archaic filler). ward = became (archaic pret. of werden); giengen (modern gingen) = went. “nach Haus” = home.

sentences.t: Den andern Morgen, wie sie unter dem finstern Thor hinaus trieben, sprach die Jungfrau
sentences.l: The next morning, as they drove out under the dark gate, the maiden spoke
sentences.i: The next morning, as they drove the geese out under the dark gate, the maiden said:
sentences.n: “den andern Morgen” = the next morning (accusative of time). finstern = inflected finster (dark). Jungfrau = maiden.

sentences.t: „o du Falada, da du hangest,“
sentences.l: “O you Falada, where you hang,”
sentences.n: The greeting repeats — the tale’s ritual return. On its recurring pattern see the first occurrence above.

sentences.t: Falada antwortete
sentences.l: Falada answered

sentences.t:
„o du Jungfer Königin, da du gangest,
wenn das deine Mutter wüßte,
das Herz thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.n: The same reply, now with das Herz (“the heart”) rather than the earlier ihr Herz — Grimm’s refrains drift slightly with each telling.

sentences.t: Und in dem Feld setzte sie sich wieder auf die Wiese und fieng an ihr Haar auszukämmen, und Kürdchen lief und wollte danach greifen, da sprach sie schnell
sentences.l: And in the field she set herself again on the meadow and began to comb out her hair, and Kürdchen ran and wanted to grab at it, then she spoke quickly
sentences.i: And in the field she sat down again on the meadow and began to comb out her hair, and Kürdchen ran up meaning to snatch at it, and she said quickly:
sentences.n: “anfangen” (fieng an; modern fing an) = to begin — separable. “auskämmen” (auszukämmen) = to comb out. “greifen nach” (danach) = to grab at, reach for.

sentences.t:
„weh, weh, Windchen,
nimm Kürdchen sein Hütchen,
und laß’n sich mit jagen,
bis ich mich geflochten und geschnatzt,
und wieder aufgesatzt.“
sentences.l:
“blow, blow, little-wind,
take Kürdchen his little-hat,
and make him chase after,
till I have braided and plaited my hair,
and set it up again.”
sentences.i:
“Blow, blow, little wind,
catch hold of Kürdchen’s hat,
and set him chasing after it,
till I have braided and bound my hair,
and put it up again.”

sentences.t: Da wehte der Wind und wehte ihm das Hütchen vom Kopf weit weg, daß Kürdchen nachlaufen mußte; und als es wieder kam, hatte sie längst ihr Haar zurecht, und es konnte keins davon erwischen; und so hüteten sie die Gänse bis es Abend ward.
sentences.l: Then the wind blew and blew the little hat far off him from his head, so that Kürdchen had to run after it; and when he came back, she had long since put her hair in order, and he could catch none of it; and so they tended the geese until it became evening.
sentences.i: Then the wind blew and blew the hat far off his head, so that Kürdchen had to run after it; and when he came back, she had long since set her hair in order, and he could catch none of it; and so they tended the geese until evening came.
sentences.n: “wegwehen” split around ihm das Hütchen vom Kopf. “zurecht haben” = to have (something) in order, ready. erwischen = to catch, seize. keins = keines (none of it).

sentences.t: Abends aber, nachdem sie heim gekommen waren, gieng Kürdchen vor den alten König und sagte „mit dem Mädchen will ich nicht länger Gänse hüten.“
sentences.l: But in the evening, after they had come home, Kürdchen went before the old king and said “with that girl I will no longer tend geese.”
sentences.i: But in the evening, after they had come home, Kürdchen went before the old king and said, “I won’t tend geese with that girl any longer.”
sentences.n: “heimkommen” (separable) = to come home. “vor den alten König gehen” = to go before / into the presence of the old king.

sentences.t: „Warum denn?“ fragte der alte König.
sentences.l: “Why then?” asked the old king.
sentences.i: “And why is that?” asked the old king.
sentences.n: denn is a flavouring particle here, softening the question — “why, then?”, “but why?”

sentences.t: „Ei, das ärgert mich den ganzen Tag.“
sentences.l: “Ei, that vexes me the whole day.”
sentences.i: “Why, it vexes me all day long.”
sentences.n: Ei = an exclamation of mild protest or surprise (“why!”, “oh!”). “den ganzen Tag” = all day (accusative of duration).

sentences.t: Da befahl ihm der alte König zu erzählen wies ihm denn mit ihr gienge.
sentences.l: Then the old king commanded him to tell how it went then with him with her.
sentences.i: Then the old king ordered him to tell how things stood between him and her.
sentences.n: “befehlen” takes the dative (ihm). wies = wie es. “wie es … mit ihr gienge” = how it went for him with her, how he fared with her; gienge is the reporting subjunctive.

sentences.t: Da sagte Kürdchen „Morgens, wenn wir unter dem finstern Thor mit der Heerde durchkommen, so ist da ein Gaulskopf an der Wand, zu dem redet sie
sentences.l: Then Kürdchen said “in the mornings, when we come through under the dark gate with the herd, there is a nag’s head on the wall, to which she speaks
sentences.i: Then Kürdchen said, “In the mornings, when we come through under the dark gate with the flock, there’s a horse’s head on the wall, and she speaks to it:
sentences.n: Heerde (1857; modern Herde) = herd, flock. Gaulskopf = a nag’s / horse’s head (Gaul = nag). “durchkommen” (separable) = to come through. The direct speech runs on into the following verse block.

sentences.t: „Falada, da du hangest,“
sentences.l: “Falada, where you hang,”
sentences.n: Kürdchen quotes the girl’s greeting — this time without the opening “o du,” as a child would half-remember it.

sentences.t: da antwortet der Kopf
sentences.l: then the head answers
sentences.n: Note the present tense antwortet: Kürdchen is describing what happens every morning, so he shifts into the timeless present.

sentences.t:
„o du Königsjungfer, da du gangest,
wenn das deine Mutter wüßte,
das Herz thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.n: Königsjungfer (“king’s maiden”) is a slight variant of the earlier Jungfer Königin — Kürdchen renders the refrain a little differently, as reported speech naturally does.

sentences.t: Und so erzählte Kürdchen weiter was auf der Gänsewiese geschähe, und wie es da dem Hut im Winde nachlaufen müßte.
sentences.l: And so Kürdchen went on telling what happened on the goose-meadow, and how he there had to run after the hat in the wind.
sentences.i: And so Kürdchen went on to tell what happened on the goose-meadow, and how he had to chase the hat there in the wind.
sentences.n: “weiter erzählen” (separable) = to go on telling. geschähe and müßte are again reporting subjunctives (what he said happened, how he said he had to run). im Winde = in the wind (old dative -e).

sentences.t: Der alte König befahl ihm den nächsten Tag wieder hinaus zu treiben, und er selbst, wie es Morgen war, setzte sich hinter das finstere Thor und hörte da wie sie mit dem Haupt des Falada sprach: und dann gieng er ihr auch nach in das Feld und barg sich in einem Busch auf der Wiese.
sentences.l: The old king commanded him to drive out again the next day, and he himself, when it was morning, set himself behind the dark gate and heard there how she spoke with the head of Falada: and then he too went after her into the field and hid himself in a bush on the meadow.
sentences.i: The old king ordered him to drive out again the next day, and when morning came the king himself sat down behind the dark gate and heard how she spoke with Falada’s head; and then he too followed her into the field and hid himself in a bush on the meadow.
sentences.n: Haupt = head (elevated, of a noble creature, beside plain Kopf). “nachgehen” (separable, + dative ihr) = to follow. “sich bergen” (barg sich) = to hide oneself. “hinaustreiben” split as hinaus zu treiben.

sentences.t: Da sah er nun bald mit seinen eigenen Augen wie die Gänsemagd und der Gänsejunge die Heerde getrieben brachte, und wie nach einer Weile sie sich setzte und ihre Haare losflocht, die strahlten von Glanz.
sentences.l: There he now soon saw with his own eyes how the goose-maid and the goose-boy brought the herd driven along, and how after a while she sat herself down and unbraided her hair, which beamed with radiance.
sentences.i: There he soon saw with his own eyes how the goose-girl and the goose-boy drove the flock along, and how after a while she sat down and unbraided her hair, which shone with radiance.
sentences.n: Gänsemagd (goose-maid, the title word) and Gänsejunge (goose-boy). “die Heerde getrieben brachte” = brought the herd driving it along, i.e. drove it up. “losflechten” (losflocht) = to unbraid, loosen. “strahlen von” = to beam / shine with.

sentences.t: Gleich sprach sie wieder
sentences.l: At once she spoke again
sentences.i: At once she spoke again:
sentences.n: gleich = at once, straightaway.

sentences.t:
„weh, weh, Windchen,
faß Kürdchen sein Hütchen,
und laß’n sich mit jagen,
bis daß ich mich geflochten und geschnatzt,
und wieder aufgesatzt.“
sentences.l:
“blow, blow, little-wind,
seize Kürdchen his little-hat,
and make him chase after,
till I have braided and plaited my hair,
and set it up again.”
sentences.i:
“Blow, blow, little wind,
take hold of Kürdchen’s hat,
and set him chasing after it,
till I have braided and bound my hair,
and put it up again.”
sentences.n: The charm’s third form: this time faß (seize) instead of nimm (take), and bis daß (till) instead of plain bis. Grimm lets the spell shift word by word each time it is spoken.

sentences.t: Da kam ein Windstoß und fuhr mit Kürdchens Hut weg, daß es weit zu laufen hatte, und die Magd kämmte und flocht ihre Locken still fort, welches der alte König alles beobachtete.
sentences.l: Then came a gust of wind and went off with Kürdchen’s hat, so that he had far to run, and the maid combed and braided her locks quietly on, all of which the old king observed.
sentences.i: Then came a gust of wind and carried off Kürdchen’s hat, so that he had far to run, while the maid quietly went on combing and braiding her locks — all of which the old king observed.
sentences.n: Windstoß = gust of wind. “wegfahren mit” (fuhr … weg) = to make off with. “fortfahren” sense in “kämmte … fort” = kept on combing. welches … alles = all of which.

sentences.t: Darauf gieng er unbemerkt zurück, und als Abends die Gänsemagd heim kam, rief er sie bei Seite, und fragte warum sie dem allem so thäte?
sentences.l: Thereupon he went back unnoticed, and when in the evening the goose-maid came home, he called her aside, and asked why she did all that so?
sentences.i: Then he went back unnoticed, and when the goose-girl came home in the evening, he called her aside and asked why she did all this.
sentences.n: “zurückgehen” (separable) = to go back. unbemerkt = unnoticed. “bei Seite rufen” = to call aside. “warum sie dem allem so thäte” = why she acted so in all this (dem allem, dative; thäte, reporting subjunctive).

sentences.t: „Das darf ich euch nicht sagen, und darf auch keinem Menschen mein Leid klagen, denn so hab ich mich unter freiem Himmel verschworen, weil ich sonst um mein Leben gekommen wäre.“
sentences.l: “That I may not tell you, and may also lament my sorrow to no person, for so have I sworn myself under the open sky, because otherwise I would have come by my life.”
sentences.i: “That I may not tell you, nor may I pour out my sorrow to any soul, for I swore so under the open sky — otherwise I should have lost my life.”
sentences.n: dürfen = to be allowed/permitted (darf … nicht = may not). “sein Leid klagen” = to lament one’s sorrow (+ dative of the one you tell). “ums Leben kommen” = to lose one’s life, perish. She keeps the very oath the false maid forced on her.

sentences.t: Er drang in sie und ließ ihr keinen Frieden, aber er konnte nichts aus ihr herausbringen.
sentences.l: He pressed in on her and left her no peace, but he could get nothing out of her.
sentences.i: He pressed her hard and gave her no peace, but he could get nothing out of her.
sentences.n: “in jemanden dringen” = to press, urge someone insistently. “keinen Frieden lassen” = to give no peace. “herausbringen” (separable) = to get out (of someone).

sentences.t: Da sprach er „wenn du mir nichts sagen willst, so klag dem Eisenofen da dein Leid,“ und gieng fort.
sentences.l: Then he spoke “if you will tell me nothing, then lament your sorrow to the iron stove there,” and went off.
sentences.i: Then he said, “If you won’t tell me anything, then pour out your sorrow to the iron stove there,” and went away.
sentences.n: Eisenofen = iron stove/oven. “klag … dein Leid” — imperative of klagen with dative dem Eisenofen (tell your grief to the stove). This “telling it to the stove” is the folk device that lets her confess while keeping her oath. “fortgehen” (gieng fort) = to go away.

sentences.t: Da kroch sie in den Eisenofen, fieng an zu jammern und zu weinen, schüttete ihr Herz aus und sprach „da sitze ich nun von aller Welt verlassen, und bin doch eine Königstochter, und eine falsche Kammerjungfer hat mich mit Gewalt dahin gebracht daß ich meine königlichen Kleider habe ablegen müssen, und hat meinen Platz bei meinem Bräutigam eingenommen, und ich muß als Gänsemagd gemeine Dienste thun.
sentences.l: Then she crept into the iron stove, began to wail and to weep, poured out her heart and spoke “here I sit now, forsaken by all the world, and am yet a king’s daughter, and a false chambermaid has brought me by force to this, that I have had to lay off my royal clothes, and has taken my place beside my bridegroom, and I must as goose-maid do common services.
sentences.i: Then she crept into the iron stove, began to wail and weep, poured out her heart and said: “Here I sit now, forsaken by all the world, and yet I am a king’s daughter; a false waiting-maid has forced me to lay aside my royal garments and has taken my place beside my bridegroom, and I must do menial service as a goose-girl.
sentences.n: “sein Herz ausschütten” (schüttete … aus, separable) = to pour out one’s heart. “mit Gewalt dahin bringen, daß …” = to force (someone) to the point that. “ablegen” = to lay aside (clothes); “einnehmen” (eingenommen) = to take, occupy (a place). gemeine Dienste = common, menial service. She confesses everything to the stove — and the king, unseen, hears it.

sentences.t: Wenn das meine Mutter wüßte, das Herz im Leib thät ihr zerspringen.“
sentences.l: If my mother knew that, the heart in her body would burst.”
sentences.i: If my mother knew this, the heart in her body would break.”
sentences.n: The blood-drops’ refrain returns a last time, now in her own mouth — closing the ring the tale opened with. im Leib = in her body (Leib, without the earlier -e).

sentences.t: Der alte König stand aber außen an der Ofenröhre, lauerte ihr zu und hörte was sie sprach.
sentences.l: But the old king stood outside at the stovepipe, listened out for her and heard what she spoke.
sentences.i: But the old king was standing outside by the stovepipe, listening for her, and heard what she said.
sentences.n: Ofenröhre = the stovepipe / flue (the sound carries out through it). “zulauern” (lauerte … zu, + dative ihr) = to lie in wait listening, eavesdrop on.

sentences.t: Da kam er wieder herein und hieß sie aus dem Ofen gehen.
sentences.l: Then he came in again and bade her go out of the stove.
sentences.i: Then he came in again and bade her come out of the stove.
sentences.n: “hereinkommen” (separable) = to come in. “heißen” + bare infinitive = to bid, order (someone to do something): hieß sie … gehen = bade her go.

sentences.t: Da wurden ihr königliche Kleider angethan, und es schien ein Wunder wie sie so schön war.
sentences.l: Then royal clothes were put on her, and it seemed a wonder how she was so beautiful.
sentences.i: Then royal garments were put on her, and it seemed a marvel how beautiful she was.
sentences.n: “antun” (angethan, 1857; modern angetan) = to put on, clothe (someone) in — here passive with dative ihr. Wunder = wonder, marvel.

sentences.t: Der alte König rief seinen Sohn und offenbarte ihm daß er die falsche Braut hätte: die wäre bloß ein Kammermädchen, die wahre aber stände hier, als die gewesene Gänsemagd.
sentences.l: The old king called his son and revealed to him that he had the false bride: that one was merely a chambermaid, but the true one stood here, as the former goose-maid.
sentences.i: The old king called his son and revealed to him that he had the false bride: she was merely a waiting-maid, but the true one stood here, the goose-girl that was.
sentences.n: offenbaren = to reveal, disclose. hätte, wäre, stände are reporting subjunctives (what the king declared). bloß = merely, only. “die gewesene Gänsemagd” = the former goose-girl (gewesen as an adjective = former, one-time).

sentences.t: Der junge König war herzensfroh, als er ihre Schönheit und Tugend erblickte, und ein großes Mahl wurde angestellt, zu dem alle Leute und guten Freunde gebeten wurden.
sentences.l: The young king was heart-glad when he beheld her beauty and virtue, and a great feast was arranged, to which all people and good friends were bidden.
sentences.i: The young king was overjoyed when he beheld her beauty and virtue, and a great feast was arranged, to which all the folk and good friends were invited.
sentences.n: herzensfroh = glad at heart, overjoyed. Mahl = feast, banquet. “anstellen” (angestellt) = to arrange, set on foot. “bitten” here in the old sense = to invite (gebeten wurden = were invited).

sentences.t: Obenan saß der Bräutigam, die Königstochter zur einen Seite und die Kammerjungfer zur andern, aber die Kammerjungfer war verblendet und erkannte jene nicht mehr in dem glänzenden Schmuck.
sentences.l: At the head sat the bridegroom, the king’s daughter on the one side and the chambermaid on the other, but the chambermaid was blinded and no longer recognized the former in the gleaming finery.
sentences.i: At the head sat the bridegroom, the king’s daughter on one side and the waiting-maid on the other; but the waiting-maid was blinded and no longer knew her in her gleaming finery.
sentences.n: obenan = at the top, at the head (of the table). verblendet = blinded, deluded. jene = that one, the former (pointing back to the princess). Schmuck = jewellery, finery.

sentences.t: Als sie nun gegessen und getrunken hatten, und gutes Muths waren, gab der alte König der Kammerfrau ein Räthsel auf, was eine solche werth wäre, die den Herrn so und so betrogen hätte, erzählte damit den ganzen Verlauf und fragte „welches Urtheils ist diese würdig?“
sentences.l: When they had now eaten and drunk, and were of good cheer, the old king set the chamber-woman a riddle: what such a one would be worth, who had deceived the lord thus and so; told with it the whole course of things and asked “of what judgement is this one worthy?”
sentences.i: When they had eaten and drunk and were in good cheer, the old king set the waiting-woman a riddle: what such a woman would deserve who had betrayed her master in such and such a way — telling in it the whole story — and asked, “What sentence does she deserve?”
sentences.n: 1857 Räthsel (Rätsel, riddle), Urtheil (Urteil, judgement/sentence). “ein Räthsel aufgeben” (separable) = to pose a riddle. “gutes Muths sein” = to be of good cheer (genitive). “werth sein” = to be worth, deserve. “welches Urtheils … würdig” — würdig takes a genitive: worthy of what sentence. The king traps her into pronouncing her own doom.

sentences.t: Da sprach die falsche Braut „die ist nichts besseres werth, als daß sie splitternackt ausgezogen und in ein Faß gesteckt wird, das inwendig mit spitzen Nägeln beschlagen ist: und zwei weiße Pferde müssen vorgespannt werden, die sie Gasse auf Gasse ab zu Tode schleifen.“
sentences.l: Then the false bride spoke “she is worth nothing better than that she be stripped stark-naked and put into a barrel that is studded inside with sharp nails: and two white horses must be harnessed before it, which drag her to death, street up, street down.”
sentences.i: Then the false bride said, “She deserves nothing better than to be stripped stark naked and put into a barrel studded inside with sharp nails, and two white horses must be harnessed to it, to drag her to death down one street and up the next.”
sentences.n: The false bride sentences herself, unknowing — Grimm does not soften it. splitternackt = stark naked. “ausziehen” (ausgezogen) = to strip; “stecken” (gesteckt) = to thrust, put. Faß = barrel, cask. inwendig = on the inside. “mit Nägeln beschlagen” = studded/fitted with nails. “vorspannen” (vorgespannt) = to harness in front. “Gasse auf, Gasse ab” = up one lane and down another. “zu Tode schleifen” = to drag to death.

sentences.t: „Das bist du,“ sprach der alte König, „und hast dein eigen Urtheil gefunden, und danach soll dir wiederfahren.“
sentences.l: “That are you,” spoke the old king, “and have found your own judgement, and according to it shall it happen to you.”
sentences.i: “That is you,” said the old king, “and you have pronounced your own sentence, and so it shall be done to you.”
sentences.n: “das bist du” = that (person) is you. dein eigen Urtheil = your own sentence (eigen uninflected before the noun, an older usage). “widerfahren” (1857 wiederfahren; + dative dir) = to befall, happen to someone.

sentences.t: Und als das Urtheil vollzogen war, vermählte sich der junge König mit seiner rechten Gemahlin, und beide beherrschten ihr Reich in Frieden und Seligkeit.
sentences.l: And when the judgement was carried out, the young king wedded himself with his true consort, and both ruled their realm in peace and blessedness.
sentences.i: And when the sentence had been carried out, the young king wedded his true bride, and the two ruled their kingdom in peace and happiness.
sentences.n: “vollziehen” (vollzogen) = to carry out, execute (a sentence). “sich vermählen mit” = to marry, wed. Seligkeit = blessedness, bliss — the fairy-tale close.
